skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Lip-sync a face to a specific audio track on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across ByteDance OmniHuman, Sync Labs sync v2/Pro, Kling lipsync, and Creatify lipsync endpoints.
skill.summary: Route lip-sync requests across multiple RunComfy endpoints, picking the right model for source video, portrait stills, or script-only inputs.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill (single SKILL.md file, 237 lines) that routes lip-sync requests to the runcomfy CLI. The static analyzer flagged 97 patterns, but all are false positives: the 'external commands' are markdown code blocks showing runcomfy CLI invocations, the 'hardcoded URLs' are documentation links to runcomfy.com or placeholder URLs (your-cdn.example), and the 'filesystem' finding references the documented ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json path with 0600 permissions. The skill explicitly constrains Bash usage to `Bash(runcomfy *)` only and includes robust security documentation covering consent, token storage, allowlisted outbound endpoints, and no telemetry.

low_findings.title: External command patterns in documentation
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md contains 51 instances of backtick-quoted commands (e.g., `runcomfy run`, `npm i -g @runcomfy/cli`). These are markdown documentation examples, not executable code. The skill's allowed-tools field explicitly restricts Bash usage to `Bash(runcomfy *)` only.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md contains 32 URLs, all of which are either documentation links to runcomfy.com model pages or placeholder URLs (https://your-cdn.example/...) in code examples. The skill's security section explicitly states outbound endpoints are allowlisted to model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net/*.runcomfy.com only.

low_findings.title: Token storage in home directory
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes the API token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600. This is a legitimate, well-documented config path with appropriate file permissions, not a hidden file attack.

content.user_title: Lip-Sync Any Video or Portrait to Audio
content.value_statement: Lip-syncing faces to audio requires choosing the right model from many options. This skill routes requests across RunComfy's lip-sync endpoints automatically, selecting Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, or Creatify based on your input type and quality needs.
actual_capabilities: Routes lip-sync requests to Sync Labs sync v2/Pro for mouth-swap on existing videos
Routes to ByteDance OmniHuman for avatar-style talking heads from portrait + audio
Routes to Kling lipsync for both audio-to-video and text-to-video workflows
Routes to Creatify lipsync for Creatify-ecosystem integrations
Generates CLI commands with proper JSON input schemas for each endpoint
Provides model selection guidance based on input type, quality tier, and budget
limitations: Does not gate user inputs for consent—operator must verify rights for real persons
Cannot lock mouth sync to a specific MP3 when using text-to-video variants (audio is regenerated)
Premium models like Sync Labs v2 Pro may have higher costs for batch jobs
Output quality depends heavily on source video/audio quality and length matching

use_cases.title: Foreign-language dubbing of brand videos
use_cases.description: Sync a translated voiceover MP3 to existing brand video footage for multi-market launches.

use_cases.title: UGC ad creation from a portrait
use_cases.description: Generate a talking-head video from a single portrait image and a product-pitch voiceover.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Script-to-video generation with synced speech
use_cases.description: Write a script and get a video with naturally synced speech audio, no audio file required.
use_cases.target_user: Video producers and explainer content creators

prompt_templates.title: Basic mouth-swap request
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a source video at https://example.com/video.mp4 and a voiceover at https://example.com/audio.mp3. Use Sync Labs sync v2 Pro to lip-sync the face to the new audio.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with a clear video+audio use case

prompt_templates.title: Avatar from portrait
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a portrait photo at https://example.com/headshot.jpg and need a voiceover at https://example.com/voiceover.mp3 to drive it. Use OmniHuman to create a talking-head video.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants avatar-style output from a still image

prompt_templates.title: Script-only generation
prompt_templates.prompt: I don't have an audio file. Here's my script: "Welcome to our product demo. Today we'll show you three key features." Generate a video with synced speech using Kling lipsync text-to-video.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a script but no audio recording

prompt_templates.title: Multi-language dub workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have one portrait at https://example.com/face.jpg and need to create the same video in 5 languages. Route to OmniHuman with these audio files: [en.mp3, es.mp3, fr.mp3, de.mp3, jp.mp3].
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user with batch multilingual requirements

output_examples.input: Lip-sync this video to a new voiceover using Sync Labs Pro
output: Generated CLI command with video_url and audio_url parameters

output_examples.input: Create a talking avatar from my portrait and this voiceover
output: Generated CLI command with image_url and audio_url
Portrait framing recommendation: head-and-shoulders works best
Result: full-body avatar video with natural speech gestures

best_practices: Match audio length to video length before processing to avoid sync drift
Use clean voiceover audio without music beds for cleaner mouth sync
Always verify consent before lip-syncing real persons' faces to new audio
Install the runcomfy CLI only via verified npm package (@runcomfy/cli)
anti_patterns: Piping arbitrary remote install scripts into a shell on the user's behalf
Using lip-sync on real public figures without explicit consent
Using text-to-video variants when you need to lock mouth to a specific MP3
Skipping audio quality isolation (voice stem extraction) for professional results

faq.question: Which model should I use for the best mouth-sync quality?
faq.answer: Sync Labs sync v2 Pro is the industry benchmark for mouth fidelity. Use it for hero-quality dubs and professional video.

faq.question: Can I use this with a portrait image instead of a video?
faq.answer: Yes. Use OmniHuman for avatar-style output from a single portrait plus audio. The model generates natural gestures and speech.

faq.question: What if I only have a script and no audio file?
faq.answer: Use Kling lipsync text-to-video or HappyHorse 1.0. Both generate audio in-pass from your script and sync it to the video.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with real people's faces?
faq.answer: The skill does not gate inputs. You must verify consent before lip-syncing real persons. The skill's documentation includes a consent section with guidance.

faq.question: Where is my API token stored?
faq.answer: After running runcomfy login, your token is stored in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. In CI, use the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: What endpoints does the CLI communicate with?
faq.answer: Only model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net/*.runcomfy.com. There is no telemetry, and a 2 GiB file size cap protects against runaway downloads.
